title: Aeneid, Book 3
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 3 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 3 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 3 (718 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>Postquam res Asiae Priamique evertere gentem <sup>2</sup>immeritam visum Superis, ceciditque superbum <sup>3</sup>Ilium, et omnis humo fumat Neptunia Troia, <sup>4</sup>diversa exsilia et desertas quaerere terras <sup>5</sup>auguriis agimur divom, classemque sub ipsa <sup>6</sup>Antandro et Phrygiae molimur montibus Idae, <sup>7</sup>incerti, quo fata ferant, ubi sistere detur, <sup>8</sup>contrahimusque viros.  Vix prima inceperat aestas, <sup>9</sup>et pater Anchises dare fatis vela iubebat; <sup>10</sup>litora cum patriae lacrimans portusque relinquo <sup>11</sup>et campos, ubi Troia fuit: feror exsul in altum <sup>12</sup>cum sociis natoque Penatibus et magnis dis. <sup>13</sup>Terra procul vastis colitur Mavortia campis, <sup>14</sup>Thraces arant, acri quondam regnata Lycurgo, <sup>15</sup>hospitium antiquum Troiae sociique Penates, <sup>16</sup>dum Fortuna fuit.  Feror huc, et litore curvo <sup>17</sup>moenia prima loco, fatis ingressus iniquis, <sup>18</sup>Aeneadasque meo nomen de nomine fingo. <sup>19</sup>Sacra Dionaeae matri divisque ferebam <sup>20</sup>auspicibus coeptorum operum, superoque nitentem <sup>21</sup>caelicolum regi mactabam in litore taurum. <sup>22</sup>Forte fuit iuxta tumulus, quo cornea summo <sup>23</sup>virgulta et densis hastilibus horrida myrtus. <sup>24</sup>Accessi, viridemque ab humo convellere silvam <sup>25</sup>conatus, ramis tegerem ut frondentibus aras,
translation.text: Aeneas proceeds in his relation: he gives an account of the fleet with which he sailed, and the success of his first voyage to Thrace. From thence he directs his course to Delos and asks the oracle what place the gods had appointed for his habitation. By a mistake of the oracle’s answer, he settles in Crete. His household gods give him the true sense of the oracle in a dream. He follows their advice, and makes the best of his way for Italy. He is cast on several shores, and meets with very surprising adventures, till at length he lands on Sicily, where his father Anchises dies. This is the place which he was sailing from, when the tempest rose, and threw him upon the Carthaginian coast.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>horrendum et dictu video mirabile monstrum. <sup>27</sup>Nam, quae prima solo ruptis radicibus arbos <sup>28</sup>vellitur, huic atro liquuntur sanguine guttae, <sup>29</sup>et terram tabo maculant.  Mihi frigidus horror <sup>30</sup>membra quatit, gelidusque coit formidine sanguis. <sup>31</sup>Rursus et alterius lentum convellere vimen <sup>32</sup>insequor, et causas penitus temptare latentis: <sup>33</sup>ater et alterius sequitur de cortice sanguis. <sup>34</sup>Multa movens animo nymphas venerabar agrestis <sup>35</sup>Gradivumque patrem, Geticis qui praesidet arvis, <sup>36</sup>rite secundarent visus omenque levarent. <sup>37</sup>Tertia sed postquam maiore hastilia nisu <sup>38</sup>adgredior, genibusque adversae obluctor harenae— <sup>39</sup>eloquar, an sileam?—gemitus lacrimabilis imo <sup>40</sup>auditur tumulo, et vox reddita fertur ad auris: <sup>41</sup>Quid miserum, Aenea, laceras?  Iam parce sepulto; <sup>42</sup>parce pias scelerare manus.  Non me tibi Troia <sup>43</sup>externum tulit, aut cruor hic de stipite manat. <sup>44</sup>Heu, fuge crudelis terras, fuge litus avarum: <sup>45</sup>nam Polydorus ego; hic confixum ferrea texit <sup>46</sup>telorum seges et iaculis increvit acutis. <sup>47</sup>Tum vero ancipiti mentem formidine pressus <sup>48</sup>obstipui, steteruntque comae et vox faucibus haesit. <sup>49</sup>Hunc Polydorum auri quondam cum pondere magno <sup>50</sup>infelix Priamus furtim mandarat alendum
translation.text: When Heav’n had overturn’d the Trojan state And Priam’s throne, by too severe a fate; When ruin’d Troy became the Grecians’ prey, And Ilium’s lofty tow’rs in ashes lay; Warn’d by celestial omens, we retreat, To seek in foreign lands a happier seat. Near old Antandros, and at Ida’s foot, The timber of the sacred groves we cut, And build our fleet; uncertain yet to find What place the gods for our repose assign’d. Friends daily flock; and scarce the kindly spring Began to clothe the ground, and birds to sing, When old Anchises summon’d all to sea: The crew my father and the Fates obey. With sighs and tears I leave my native shore, And empty fields, where Ilium stood before. My sire, my son, our less and greater gods, All sail at once, and cleave the briny floods.

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>Threicio regi, cum iam diffideret armis <sup>52</sup>Dardaniae, cingique urbem obsidione videret. <sup>53</sup>Ille, ut opes fractae Teucrum, et Fortuna recessit, <sup>54</sup>res Agamemnonias victriciaque arma secutus, <sup>55</sup>fas omne abrumpit; Polydorum obtruncat, et auro <sup>56</sup>vi potitur.  Quid non mortalia pectora cogis, <sup>57</sup>auri sacra fames?  Postquam pavor ossa reliquit, <sup>58</sup>delectos populi ad proceres primumque parentem <sup>59</sup>monstra deum refero, et quae sit sententia posco. <sup>60</sup>Omnibus idem animus, scelerata excedere terra, <sup>61</sup>linqui pollutum hospitium, et dare classibus austros. <sup>62</sup>Ergo instauramus Polydoro funus, et ingens <sup>63</sup>aggeritur tumulo tellus; stant Manibus arae, <sup>64</sup>caeruleis maestae vittis atraque cupresso, <sup>65</sup>et circum Iliades crinem de more solutae; <sup>66</sup>inferimus tepido spumantia cymbia lacte <sup>67</sup>sanguinis et sacri pateras, animamque sepulchro <sup>68</sup>condimus, et magna supremum voce ciemus. <sup>69</sup>Inde, ubi prima fides pelago, placataque venti <sup>70</sup>dant maria et lenis crepitans vocat Auster in altum, <sup>71</sup>deducunt socii navis et litora complent: <sup>72</sup>provehimur portu, terraeque urbesque recedunt. <sup>73</sup>Sacra mari colitur medio gratissima tellus <sup>74</sup>Nereidum matri et Neptuno Aegaeo, <sup>75</sup>quam pius arquitenens oras et litora circum
translation.text: “Against our coast appears a spacious land, Which once the fierce Lycurgus did command, Thracia the name; the people bold in war; Vast are their fields, and tillage is their care, A hospitable realm while Fate was kind, With Troy in friendship and religion join’d. I land; with luckless omens, then adore Their gods, and draw a line along the shore; I lay the deep foundations of a wall, And Aenos, nam’d from me, the city call. To Dionaean Venus vows are paid, And all the pow’rs that rising labours aid; A bull on Jove’s imperial altar laid. Not far, a rising hillock stood in view; Sharp myrtles on the sides, and cornels grew. There, while I went to crop the sylvan scenes, And shade our altar with their leafy greens, I pull’d a plant; with horror I relate A prodigy so strange and full of fate. The rooted fibers rose, and from the wound Black bloody drops distill’d upon the ground. Mute and amaz’d, my hair with terror stood; Fear shrunk my sinews, and congeal’d my blood. Mann’d once again, another plant I try: That other gush’d with the same sanguine dye. Then, fearing guilt for some offence unknown, With pray’rs and vows the Dryads I atone, With all the sisters of the woods, and most The God of Arms, who rules the Thracian coast, That they, or he, these omens would avert, Release our fears, and better signs impart. Clear’d, as I thought, and fully fix’d at length To learn the cause, I tugged with all my strength: I bent my knees against the ground; once more The violated myrtle ran with gore. Scarce dare I tell the sequel: from the womb Of wounded earth, and caverns of the tomb, A groan, as of a troubled ghost, renew’d My fright, and then these dreadful words ensued: ‘Why dost thou thus my buried body rend? O spare the corpse of thy unhappy friend! Spare to pollute thy pious hands with blood: The tears distil not from the wounded wood; But ev’ry drop this living tree contains Is kindred blood, and ran in Trojan veins. O fly from this unhospitable shore, Warn’d by my fate; for I am Polydore! Here loads of lances, in my blood embrued, Again shoot upward, by my blood renew’d.’

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>errantem Mycono e celsa Gyaroque revinxit, <sup>77</sup>immotamque coli dedit et contemnere ventos. <sup>78</sup>Huc feror; haec fessos tuto placidissima portu <sup>79</sup>accipit: egressi veneramur Apollinis urbem. <sup>80</sup>Rex Anius, rex idem hominum Phoebique sacerdos <sup>81</sup>vittis et sacra redimitus tempora lauro, <sup>82</sup>occurrit; veterem Anchisen adgnovit amicum. <sup>83</sup>Iungimus hospitio dextras, et tecta subimus. <sup>84</sup>Templa dei saxo venerabar structa vetusto: <sup>85</sup>Da propriam, Thymbraee, domum; da moenia fessis <sup>86</sup>et genus et mansuram urbem; serva altera Troiae <sup>87</sup>Pergama, reliquias Danaum atque immitis Achilli. <sup>88</sup>Quem sequimur?  Quove ire iubes?  Ubi ponere sedes? <sup>89</sup>Da, pater, augurium, atque animis inlabere nostris. <sup>90</sup>Vix ea fatus eram: tremere omnia visa repente, <sup>91</sup>liminaque laurusque dei, totusque moveri <sup>92</sup>mons circum, et mugire adytis cortina reclusis. <sup>93</sup>Submissi petimus terram, et vox fertur ad auris: <sup>94</sup>Dardanidae duri, quae vos a stirpe parentum <sup>95</sup>prima tulit tellus, eadem vos ubere laeto <sup>96</sup>accipiet reduces.  Antiquam exquirite matrem: <sup>97</sup>hic domus Aeneae cunctis dominabitur oris, <sup>98</sup>et nati natorum, et qui nascentur ab illis. <sup>99</sup>Haec Phoebus; mixtoque ingens exorta tumultu <sup>100</sup>laetitia, et cuncti quae sint ea moenia quaerunt,
translation.text: “My falt’ring tongue and shiv’ring limbs declare My horror, and in bristles rose my hair. When Troy with Grecian arms was closely pent, Old Priam, fearful of the war’s event, This hapless Polydore to Thracia sent: Loaded with gold, he sent his darling, far From noise and tumults, and destructive war, Committed to the faithless tyrant’s care; Who, when he saw the pow’r of Troy decline, Forsook the weaker, with the strong to join; Broke ev’ry bond of nature and of truth, And murder’d, for his wealth, the royal youth. O sacred hunger of pernicious gold! What bands of faith can impious lucre hold? Now, when my soul had shaken off her fears, I call my father and the Trojan peers; Relate the prodigies of Heav’n, require What he commands, and their advice desire. All vote to leave that execrable shore, Polluted with the blood of Polydore; But, ere we sail, his fun’ral rites prepare, Then, to his ghost, a tomb and altars rear. In mournful pomp the matrons walk the round, With baleful cypress and blue fillets crown’d, With eyes dejected, and with hair unbound. Then bowls of tepid milk and blood we pour, And thrice invoke the soul of Polydore.

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>quo Phoebus vocet errantis iubeatque reverti? <sup>102</sup>Tum genitor, veterum volvens monumenta virorum, <sup>103</sup>Audite, O proceres ait et spes discite vestras: <sup>104</sup>Creta Iovis magni medio iacet insula ponto; <sup>105</sup>mons Idaeus ubi, et gentis cunabula nostrae. <sup>106</sup>Centum urbes habitant magnas, uberrima regna; <sup>107</sup>maximus unde pater, si rite audita recordor, <sup>108</sup>Teucrus Rhoeteas primum est advectus in oras, <sup>109</sup>optavitque locum regno.  Nondum Ilium et arces <sup>110</sup>Pergameae steterant; habitabant vallibus imis. <sup>111</sup>hinc mater cultrix Cybeli Corybantiaque aera <sup>112</sup>Idaeumque nemus; hinc fida silentia sacris, <sup>113</sup>et iuncti currum dominae subiere leones. <sup>114</sup>Ergo agite, et, divom ducunt qua iussa, sequamur; <sup>115</sup>placemus ventos et Gnosia regna petamus. <sup>116</sup>Nec longo distant cursu; modo Iuppiter adsit, <sup>117</sup>tertia lux classem Cretaeis sistet in oris. <sup>118</sup>Sic fatus, meritos aris mactavit honores, <sup>119</sup>taurum Neptuno, taurum tibi, pulcher Apollo <sup>120</sup>nigram Hiemi pecudem, Zephyris felicibus albam. <sup>121</sup>Fama volat pulsum regnis cessisse paternis <sup>122</sup>Idomenea ducem, desertaque litora Cretae <sup>123</sup>hoste vacare domos, sedesque adstare relictas. <sup>124</sup>Linquimus Ortygiae portus, pelagoque volamus, <sup>125</sup>bacchatamque iugis Naxon viridemque Donysam,
translation.text:
“Now, when the raging storms no longer reign, But southern gales invite us to the main, We launch our vessels, with a prosp’rous wind, And leave the cities and the shores behind.

“An island in th’ Aegaean main appears; Neptune and wat’ry Doris claim it theirs. It floated once, till Phoebus fix’d the sides To rooted earth, and now it braves the tides. Here, borne by friendly winds, we come ashore, With needful ease our weary limbs restore, And the Sun’s temple and his town adore.

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>Olearon, niveamque Paron, sparsasque per aequor <sup>127</sup>Cycladas, et crebris legimus freta consita terris. <sup>128</sup>Nauticus exoritur vario certamine clamor; <sup>129</sup>hortantur socii: Cretam proavosque petamus! <sup>130</sup>Prosequitur surgens a puppi ventus euntis <sup>131</sup>et tandem antiquis Curetum adlabimur oris. <sup>132</sup>Ergo avidus muros optatae molior urbis, <sup>133</sup>Pergameamque voco, et laetam cognomine gentem <sup>134</sup>hortor amare focos arcemque attollere tectis. <sup>135</sup>Iamque fere sicco subductae litore puppes; <sup>136</sup>conubiis arvisque novis operata iuventus; <sup>137</sup>iura domosque dabam: subito cum tabida membris, <sup>138</sup>corrupto caeli tractu, miserandaque venit <sup>139</sup>arboribusque satisque lues et letifer annus. <sup>140</sup>Linquebant dulcis animas, aut aegra trahebant <sup>141</sup>corpora; tum sterilis exurere Sirius agros; <sup>142</sup>arebant herbae, et victum seges aegra negabat. <sup>143</sup>Rursus ad oraclum Ortygiae Phoebumque remenso <sup>144</sup>hortatur pater ire mari, veniamque precari: <sup>145</sup>quam fessis finem rebus ferat; unde laborum <sup>146</sup>temptare auxilium iubeat; quo vertere cursus. <sup>147</sup>Nox erat, et terris animalia somnus habebat: <sup>148</sup>effigies sacrae divom Phrygiique Penates, <sup>149</sup>quos mecum a Troia mediisque ex ignibus urbis <sup>150</sup>extuleram, visi ante oculos adstare iacentis
translation.text: “Anius, the priest and king, with laurel crown’d, His hoary locks with purple fillets bound, Who saw my sire the Delian shore ascend, Came forth with eager haste to meet his friend; Invites him to his palace; and, in sign Of ancient love, their plighted hands they join. Then to the temple of the god I went, And thus, before the shrine, my vows present: ‘Give, O Thymbraeus, give a resting place To the sad relics of the Trojan race; A seat secure, a region of their own, A lasting empire, and a happier town. Where shall we fix? where shall our labours end? Whom shall we follow, and what fate attend? Let not my pray’rs a doubtful answer find; But in clear auguries unveil thy mind.’ Scarce had I said: he shook the holy ground, The laurels, and the lofty hills around; And from the tripos rush’d a bellowing sound. Prostrate we fell; confess’d the present god, Who gave this answer from his dark abode: ‘Undaunted youths, go, seek that mother earth From which your ancestors derive their birth. The soil that sent you forth, her ancient race In her old bosom shall again embrace. Through the wide world th’ Aeneian house shall reign, And children’s children shall the crown sustain.’ Thus Phoebus did our future fates disclose: A mighty tumult, mix’d with joy, arose.

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>in somnis, multo manifesti lumine, qua se <sup>152</sup>plena per insertas fundebat luna fenestras; <sup>153</sup>tum sic adfari et curas his demere dictis: <sup>154</sup>Quod tibi delato Ortygiam dicturus Apollo est, <sup>155</sup>hic canit, et tua nos en ultro ad limina mittit. <sup>156</sup>Nos te, Dardania incensa, tuaque arma secuti, <sup>157</sup>nos tumidum sub te permensi classibus aequor, <sup>158</sup>idem venturos tollemus in astra nepotes, <sup>159</sup>imperiumque urbi dabimus: tu moenia magnis <sup>160</sup>magna para, longumque fugae ne linque laborem. <sup>161</sup>Mutandae sedes: non haec tibi litora suasit <sup>162</sup>Delius, aut Cretae iussit considere Apollo. <sup>163</sup>Est locus, Hesperiam Grai cognomine dicunt, <sup>164</sup>terra antiqua, potens armis atque ubere glaebae; <sup>165</sup>Oenotri coluere viri; nunc fama minores <sup>166</sup>Italiam dixisse ducis de nomine gentem: <sup>167</sup>hae nobis propriae sedes; hinc Dardanus ortus, <sup>168</sup>Iasiusque pater, genus a quo principe nostrum. <sup>169</sup>Surge age, et haec laetus longaevo dicta parenti <sup>170</sup>haud dubitanda refer: Corythum terrasque requirat <sup>171</sup>Ausonias; Dictaea negat tibi Iuppiter arva. <sup>172</sup>Talibus attonitus visis et voce deorum— <sup>173</sup>nec sopor illud erat, sed coram adgnoscere voltus <sup>174</sup>velatasque comas praesentiaque ora videbar; <sup>175</sup>tum gelidus toto manabat corpore sudor
translation.text: “All are concern’d to know what place the god Assign’d, and where determin’d our abode. My father, long revolving in his mind The race and lineage of the Trojan kind, Thus answer’d their demands: ‘Ye princes, hear Your pleasing fortune, and dispel your fear. The fruitful isle of Crete, well known to fame, Sacred of old to Jove’s imperial name, In the mid ocean lies, with large command, And on its plains a hundred cities stand. Another Ida rises there, and we From thence derive our Trojan ancestry. From thence, as ’tis divulg’d by certain fame, To the Rhoetean shores old Teucrus came; There fix’d, and there the seat of empire chose, Ere Ilium and the Trojan tow’rs arose. In humble vales they built their soft abodes, Till Cybele, the mother of the gods, With tinkling cymbals charm’d th’ Idaean woods, She secret rites and ceremonies taught, And to the yoke the savage lions brought. Let us the land which Heav’n appoints, explore; Appease the winds, and seek the Gnossian shore. If Jove assists the passage of our fleet, The third propitious dawn discovers Crete.’ Thus having said, the sacrifices, laid On smoking altars, to the gods he paid: A bull, to Neptune an oblation due, Another bull to bright Apollo slew; A milk-white ewe, the western winds to please, And one coal-black, to calm the stormy seas. Ere this, a flying rumour had been spread That fierce Idomeneus from Crete was fled, Expell’d and exil’d; that the coast was free From foreign or domestic enemy.

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>corripio e stratis corpus, tendoque supinas <sup>177</sup>ad caelum cum voce manus, et munera libo <sup>178</sup>intemerata focis.  Perfecto laetus honore <sup>179</sup>Anchisen facio certum, remque ordine pando. <sup>180</sup>Adgnovit prolem ambiguam geminosque parentes, <sup>181</sup>seque novo veterum deceptum errore locorum. <sup>182</sup>Tum memorat: Nate, Iliacis exercite fatis, <sup>183</sup>sola mihi talis casus Cassandra canebat. <sup>184</sup>Nunc repeto haec generi portendere debita nostro, <sup>185</sup>et saepe Hesperiam, saepe Itala regna vocare. <sup>186</sup>Sed quis ad Hesperiae venturos litora Teucros <sup>187</sup>crederet, aut quem tum vates Cassandra moveret? <sup>188</sup>Cedamus Phoebo, et moniti meliora sequamur. <sup>189</sup>Sic ait, et cuncti dicto paremus ovantes. <sup>190</sup>Hanc quoque deserimus sedem, paucisque relictis <sup>191</sup>vela damus, vastumque cava trabe currimus aequor. <sup>192</sup>Postquam altum tenuere rates, nec iam amplius ullae <sup>193</sup>adparent terrae, caelum undique et undique pontus, <sup>194</sup>tum mihi caeruleus supra caput adstitit imber, <sup>195</sup>noctem hiememque ferens, et inhorruit unda tenebris. <sup>196</sup>Continuo venti volvunt mare, magnaque surgunt <sup>197</sup>aequora; dispersi iactamur gurgite vasto; <sup>198</sup>involvere diem nimbi, et nox umida caelum <sup>199</sup>abstulit; ingeminant abruptis nubibus ignes. <sup>200</sup>Excutimur cursu, et caecis erramus in undis.
translation.text: “We leave the Delian ports, and put to sea. By Naxos, fam’d for vintage, make our way; Then green Donysa pass; and sail in sight Of Paros’ isle, with marble quarries white. We pass the scatter’d isles of Cyclades, That, scarce distinguish’d, seem to stud the seas. The shouts of sailors double near the shores; They stretch their canvas, and they ply their oars. ‘All hands aloft! for Crete! for Crete!’ they cry, And swiftly thro’ the foamy billows fly. Full on the promis’d land at length we bore, With joy descending on the Cretan shore. With eager haste a rising town I frame, Which from the Trojan Pergamus I name: The name itself was grateful; I exhort To found their houses, and erect a fort. Our ships are haul’d upon the yellow strand; The youth begin to till the labour’d land; And I myself new marriages promote, Give laws, and dwellings I divide by lot; When rising vapours choke the wholesome air, And blasts of noisome winds corrupt the year; The trees devouring caterpillars burn; Parch’d was the grass, and blighted was the corn: Nor ’scape the beasts; for Sirius, from on high, With pestilential heat infects the sky: My men, some fall, the rest in fevers fry. Again my father bids me seek the shore Of sacred Delos, and the god implore, To learn what end of woes we might expect, And to what clime our weary course direct.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>Ipse diem noctemque negat discernere caelo, <sup>202</sup>nec meminisse viae media Palinurus in unda. <sup>203</sup>Tris adeo incertos caeca caligine soles <sup>204</sup>erramus pelago, totidem sine sidere noctes. <sup>205</sup>Quarto terra die primum se attollere tandem <sup>206</sup>visa, aperire procul montis, ac volvere fumum. <sup>207</sup>Vela cadunt, remis insurgimus; haud mora nautae <sup>208</sup>adnixi torquent spumas et caerula verrunt. <sup>209</sup>Servatum ex undis Strophadum me litora primum <sup>210</sup>accipiunt; Strophades Graio stant nomine dictae, <sup>211</sup>insulae Ionio in magno, quas dira Celaeno <sup>212</sup>Harpyiaeque colunt aliae, Phineia postquam <sup>213</sup>clausa domus, mensasque metu liquere priores. <sup>214</sup>Tristius haud illis monstrum, nec saevior ulla <sup>215</sup>pestis et ira deum Stygiis sese extulit undis. <sup>216</sup>Virginei volucrum voltus, foedissima ventris <sup>217</sup>proluvies, uncaeque manus, et pallida semper <sup>218</sup>ora fame. <sup>219</sup>Huc ubi delati portus intravimus, ecce <sup>220</sup>laeta boum passim campis armenta videmus, <sup>221</sup>caprigenumque pecus nullo custode per herbas. <sup>222</sup>Inruimus ferro, et divos ipsumque vocamus <sup>223</sup>in partem praedamque Iovem; tum litore curvo <sup>224</sup>exstruimusque toros, dapibusque epulamur opimis. <sup>225</sup>At subitae horrifico lapsu de montibus adsunt
translation.text: “’Twas night, when ev’ry creature, void of cares, The common gift of balmy slumber shares: The statues of my gods (for such they seem’d), Those gods whom I from flaming Troy redeem’d, Before me stood, majestically bright, Full in the beams of Phoebe’s ent’ring light. Then thus they spoke, and eas’d my troubled mind: ‘What from the Delian god thou go’st to find, He tells thee here, and sends us to relate. Those pow’rs are we, companions of thy fate, Who from the burning town by thee were brought, Thy fortune follow’d, and thy safety wrought. Thro’ seas and lands as we thy steps attend, So shall our care thy glorious race befriend. An ample realm for thee thy fates ordain, A town that o’er the conquer’d world shall reign. Thou, mighty walls for mighty nations build; Nor let thy weary mind to labours yield: But change thy seat; for not the Delian god, Nor we, have giv’n thee Crete for our abode. A land there is, Hesperia call’d of old, The soil is fruitful, and the natives bold. Th’ Oenotrians held it once, by later fame Now call’d Italia, from the leader’s name. Jasius there and Dardanus were born; From thence we came, and thither must return. Rise, and thy sire with these glad tidings greet. Search Italy; for Jove denies thee Crete.’

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>Harpyiae, et magnis quatiunt clangoribus alas, <sup>227</sup>diripiuntque dapes, contactuque omnia foedant <sup>228</sup>immundo; tum vox taetrum dira inter odorem. <sup>229</sup>Rursum in secessu longo sub rupe cavata, <sup>230</sup>arboribus clausi circum atque horrentibus umbris, <sup>231</sup>instruimus mensas arisque reponimus ignem: <sup>232</sup>rursum ex diverso caeli caecisque latebris <sup>233</sup>turba sonans praedam pedibus circumvolat uncis, <sup>234</sup>polluit ore dapes.  Sociis tunc, arma capessant, <sup>235</sup>edico, et dira bellum cum gente gerendum. <sup>236</sup>Haud secus ac iussi faciunt, tectosque per herbam <sup>237</sup>disponunt enses et scuta latentia condunt. <sup>238</sup>Ergo ubi delapsae sonitum per curva dedere <sup>239</sup>litora, dat signum specula Misenus ab alta <sup>240</sup>aere cavo.  Invadunt socii, et nova proelia temptant, <sup>241</sup>obscenas pelagi ferro foedare volucres: <sup>242</sup>sed neque vim plumis ullam nec volnera tergo <sup>243</sup>accipiunt, celerique fuga sub sidera lapsae <sup>244</sup>semesam praedam et vestigia foeda relinquunt. <sup>245</sup>Una in praecelsa consedit rupe Celaeno, <sup>246</sup>infelix vates, rumpitque hanc pectore vocem: <sup>247</sup>Bellum etiam pro caede boum stratisque iuvencis, <sup>248</sup>Laomedontiadae, bellumne inferre paratis, <sup>249</sup>et patrio Harpyias insontis pellere regno? <sup>250</sup>Accipite ergo animis atque haec mea figite dicta,
translation.text:
“Astonish’d at their voices and their sight, (Nor were they dreams, but visions of the night; I saw, I knew their faces, and descried, In perfect view, their hair with fillets tied;) I started from my couch; a clammy sweat On all my limbs and shiv’ring body sate. To heav’n I lift my hands with pious haste, And sacred incense in the flames I cast. Thus to the gods their perfect honours done, More cheerful, to my good old sire I run, And tell the pleasing news. In little space He found his error of the double race; Not, as before he deem’d, deriv’d from Crete; No more deluded by the doubtful seat: Then said: ‘O son, turmoil’d in Trojan fate! Such things as these Cassandra did relate. This day revives within my mind what she Foretold of Troy renew’d in Italy, And Latian lands; but who could then have thought That Phrygian gods to Latium should be brought, Or who believ’d what mad Cassandra taught? Now let us go where Phoebus leads the way.’

“He said; and we with glad consent obey, Forsake the seat, and, leaving few behind, We spread our sails before the willing wind. Now from the sight of land our galleys move, With only seas around and skies above; When o’er our heads descends a burst of rain, And night with sable clouds involves the main; The ruffling winds the foamy billows raise; The scatter’d fleet is forc’d to sev’ral ways; The face of heav’n is ravish’d from our eyes, And in redoubled peals the roaring thunder flies. Cast from our course, we wander in the dark. No stars to guide, no point of land to mark. Ev’n Palinurus no distinction found Betwixt the night and day; such darkness reign’d around. Three starless nights the doubtful navy strays, Without distinction, and three sunless days; The fourth renews the light, and, from our shrouds, We view a rising land, like distant clouds; The mountain-tops confirm the pleasing sight, And curling smoke ascending from their height. The canvas falls; their oars the sailors ply; From the rude strokes the whirling waters fly. At length I land upon the Strophades, Safe from the danger of the stormy seas. Those isles are compass’d by th’ Ionian main, The dire abode where the foul Harpies reign, Forc’d by the winged warriors to repair To their old homes, and leave their costly fare. Monsters more fierce offended Heav’n ne’er sent From hell’s abyss, for human punishment: With virgin faces, but with wombs obscene, Foul paunches, and with ordure still unclean; With claws for hands, and looks for ever lean.

translation.original_text: <sup>251</sup>quae Phoebo pater omnipotens, mihi Phoebus Apollo <sup>252</sup>praedixit, vobis Furiarum ego maxuma pando. <sup>253</sup>Italiam cursu petitis, ventisque vocatis <sup>254</sup>ibitis Italiam, portusque intrare licebit; <sup>255</sup>sed non ante datam cingetis moenibus urbem, <sup>256</sup>quam vos dira fames nostraeque iniuria caedis <sup>257</sup>ambesas subigat malis absumere mensas. <sup>258</sup>Dixit, et in silvam pennis ablata refugit. <sup>259</sup>At sociis subita gelidus formidine sanguis <sup>260</sup>deriguit; cecidere animi, nec iam amplius armis, <sup>261</sup>sed votis precibusque iubent exposcere pacem, <sup>262</sup>sive deae, seu sint dirae obscenaeque volucres. <sup>263</sup>Et pater Anchises passis de litore palmis <sup>264</sup>numina magna vocat, meritosque indicit honores: <sup>265</sup>Di, prohibete minas; di, talem avertite casum, <sup>266</sup>et placidi servate pios! Tum litore funem <sup>267</sup>deripere, excussosque iubet laxare rudentes. <sup>268</sup>Tendunt vela Noti; fugimus spumantibus undis, <sup>269</sup>qua cursum ventusque gubernatorque vocabat. <sup>270</sup>Iam medio adparet fluctu nemorosa Zacynthos <sup>271</sup>Dulichiumque Sameque et Neritos ardua saxis. <sup>272</sup>Effugimus scopulos Ithacae, Laertia regna, <sup>273</sup>et terram altricem saevi exsecramur Ulixi. <sup>274</sup>Mox et Leucatae nimbosa cacumina montis <sup>275</sup>et formidatus nautis aperitur Apollo.
translation.text: “We landed at the port, and soon beheld Fat herds of oxen graze the flow’ry field, And wanton goats without a keeper stray’d. With weapons we the welcome prey invade, Then call the gods for partners of our feast, And Jove himself, the chief invited guest. We spread the tables on the greensward ground; We feed with hunger, and the bowls go round; When from the mountain-tops, with hideous cry, And clatt’ring wings, the hungry Harpies fly; They snatch the meat, defiling all they find, And, parting, leave a loathsome stench behind. Close by a hollow rock, again we sit, New dress the dinner, and the beds refit, Secure from sight, beneath a pleasing shade, Where tufted trees a native arbour made. Again the holy fires on altars burn; And once again the rav’nous birds return, Or from the dark recesses where they lie, Or from another quarter of the sky; With filthy claws their odious meal repeat, And mix their loathsome ordures with their meat. I bid my friends for vengeance then prepare, And with the hellish nation wage the war. They, as commanded, for the fight provide, And in the grass their glitt’ring weapons hide; Then, when along the crooked shore we hear Their clatt’ring wings, and saw the foes appear, Misenus sounds a charge: we take th’ alarm, And our strong hands with swords and bucklers arm. In this new kind of combat all employ Their utmost force, the monsters to destroy. In vain, the fated skin is proof to wounds; And from their plumes the shining sword rebounds. At length rebuff’d, they leave their mangled prey, And their stretch’d pinions to the skies display. Yet one remain’d, the messenger of Fate: High on a craggy cliff Celaeno sate, And thus her dismal errand did relate: ‘What! not contented with our oxen slain, Dare you with Heav’n an impious war maintain, And drive the Harpies from their native reign? Heed therefore what I say; and keep in mind What Jove decrees, what Phoebus has design’d, And I, the Furies’ queen, from both relate: You seek th’ Italian shores, foredoom’d by fate: Th’ Italian shores are granted you to find, And a safe passage to the port assign’d. But know, that ere your promis’d walls you build, My curses shall severely be fulfill’d. Fierce famine is your lot for this misdeed, Reduc’d to grind the plates on which you feed.’ She said, and to the neighb’ring forest flew. Our courage fails us, and our fears renew. Hopeless to win by war, to pray’rs we fall, And on th’ offended Harpies humbly call, And whether gods or birds obscene they were, Our vows for pardon and for peace prefer. But old Anchises, off’ring sacrifice, And lifting up to heav’n his hands and eyes, Ador’d the greater gods: ‘Avert,’ said he, ‘These omens; render vain this prophecy, And from th’ impending curse a pious people free!’

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>Hunc petimus fessi et parvae succedimus urbi; <sup>277</sup>ancora de prora iacitur, stant litore puppes. <sup>278</sup>Ergo insperata tandem tellure potiti, <sup>279</sup>lustramurque Iovi votisque incendimus aras, <sup>280</sup>Actiaque Iliacis celebramus litora ludis. <sup>281</sup>Exercent patrias oleo labente palaestras <sup>282</sup>nudati socii; iuvat evasisse tot urbes <sup>283</sup>Argolicas, mediosque fugam tenuisse per hostis. <sup>284</sup>Interea magnum sol circumvolvitur annum, <sup>285</sup>et glacialis hiemps aquilonibus asperat undas. <sup>286</sup>Aere cavo clipeum.  magni gestamen Abantis, <sup>287</sup>postibus adversis figo, et rem carmine signo: <sup>288</sup>AENEAS HAEC DE DANAIS VICTORIBVS ARMA. <sup>289</sup>Linquere tum portus iubeo et considere transtris: <sup>290</sup>certatim socii feriunt mare et aequora verrunt. <sup>291</sup>Protinus aërias Phaeacum abscondimus arces, <sup>292</sup>litoraque Epiri legimus portuque subimus <sup>293</sup>Chaonio, et celsam Buthroti accedimus urbem. <sup>294</sup>Hic incredibilis rerum fama occupat auris, <sup>295</sup>Priamiden Helenum Graias regnare per urbes, <sup>296</sup>coniugio Aeacidae Pyrrhi sceptrisque potitum, <sup>297</sup>et patrio Andromachen iterum cessisse marito. <sup>298</sup>Obstipui, miroque incensum pectus amore, <sup>299</sup>compellare virum et casus cognoscere tantos. <sup>300</sup>Progredior portu, classis et litora linquens,
translation.text: “Thus having said, he bids us put to sea; We loose from shore our haulsers, and obey, And soon with swelling sails pursue the wat’ry way. Amidst our course, Zacynthian woods appear; And next by rocky Neritos we steer: We fly from Ithaca’s detested shore, And curse the land which dire Ulysses bore. At length Leucate’s cloudy top appears, And the Sun’s temple, which the sailor fears. Resolv’d to breathe a while from labour past, Our crooked anchors from the prow we cast, And joyful to the little city haste. Here, safe beyond our hopes, our vows we pay To Jove, the guide and patron of our way. The customs of our country we pursue, And Trojan games on Actian shores renew. Our youth their naked limbs besmear with oil, And exercise the wrastlers’ noble toil; Pleas’d to have sail’d so long before the wind, And left so many Grecian towns behind. The sun had now fulfill’d his annual course, And Boreas on the seas display’d his force: I fix’d upon the temple’s lofty door The brazen shield which vanquish’d Abas bore; The verse beneath my name and action speaks: ‘These arms Aeneas took from conqu’ring Greeks.’ Then I command to weigh; the seamen ply Their sweeping oars; the smoking billows fly. The sight of high Phaeacia soon we lost, And skimm’d along Epirus’ rocky coast.

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>sollemnis cum forte dapes et tristia dona <sup>302</sup>ante urbem in luco falsi Simoentis ad undam <sup>303</sup>libabat cineri Andromache, Manisque vocabat <sup>304</sup>Hectoreum ad tumulum, viridi quem caespite inanem <sup>305</sup>et geminas, causam lacrimis, sacraverat aras. <sup>306</sup>Ut me conspexit venientem et Troïa circum <sup>307</sup>arma amens vidit, magnis exterrita monstris <sup>308</sup>deriguit visu in medio, calor ossa reliquit; <sup>309</sup>labitur, et longo vix tandem tempore fatur: <sup>310</sup>Verane te facies, verus mihi nuntius adfers, <sup>311</sup>nate dea?  Vivisne, aut, si lux alma recessit, <sup>312</sup>Hector ubi est? Dixit, lacrimasque effudit et omnem <sup>313</sup>implevit clamore locum.  Vix pauca furenti <sup>314</sup>subicio, et raris turbatus vocibus hisco: <sup>315</sup>Vivo equidem, vitamque extrema per omnia duco; <sup>316</sup>ne dubita, nam vera vides. <sup>317</sup>Heu, quis te casus deiectam coniuge tanto <sup>318</sup>excipit, aut quae digna satis fortuna revisit <sup>319</sup>Hectoris Andromachen?  Pyrrhin’ conubia servas? <sup>320</sup>Deiecit vultum et demissa voce locuta est: <sup>321</sup>O felix una ante alias Priameïa virgo, <sup>322</sup>hostilem ad tumulum Troiae sub moenibus altis <sup>323</sup>iussa mori, quae sortitus non pertulit ullos, <sup>324</sup>nec victoris eri tetigit captiva cubile! <sup>325</sup>nos, patria incensa, diversa per aequora vectae,
translation.text: “Then to Chaonia’s port our course we bend, And, landed, to Buthrotus’ heights ascend. Here wondrous things were loudly blaz’d fame: How Helenus reviv’d the Trojan name, And reign’d in Greece; that Priam’s captive son Succeeded Pyrrhus in his bed and throne; And fair Andromache, restor’d by fate, Once more was happy in a Trojan mate. I leave my galleys riding in the port, And long to see the new Dardanian court. By chance, the mournful queen, before the gate, Then solemniz’d her former husband’s fate. Green altars, rais’d of turf, with gifts she crown’d, And sacred priests in order stand around, And thrice the name of hapless Hector sound. The grove itself resembles Ida’s wood; And Simois seem’d the well-dissembled flood. But when at nearer distance she beheld My shining armour and my Trojan shield, Astonish’d at the sight, the vital heat Forsakes her limbs; her veins no longer beat: She faints, she falls, and scarce recov’ring strength, Thus, with a falt’ring tongue, she speaks at length:

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>stirpis Achilleae fastus iuvenemque superbum, <sup>327</sup>servitio enixae, tulimus: qui deinde, secutus <sup>328</sup>Ledaeam Hermionen Lacedaemoniosque hymenaeos, <sup>329</sup>me famulo famulamque Heleno transmisit habendam. <sup>330</sup>Ast illum, ereptae magno inflammatus amore <sup>331</sup>coniugis et scelerum Furiis agitatus, Orestes <sup>332</sup>excipit incautum patriasque obtruncat ad aras. <sup>333</sup>Morte Neoptolemi regnorum reddita cessit <sup>334</sup>pars Heleno, qui Chaonios cognomine campos <sup>335</sup>Chaoniamque omnem Troiano a Chaone dixit, <sup>336</sup>Pergamaque Iliacamque iugis hanc addidit arcem. <sup>337</sup>Sed tibi qui cursum venti, quae fata dedere? <sup>338</sup>Aut quisnam ignarum nostris deus adpulit oris? <sup>339</sup>Quid puer Ascanius?  superatne et vescitur aura, <sup>340</sup>quem tibi iam Troia— <sup>341</sup>Ecqua tamen puero est amissae cura parentis? <sup>342</sup>Ecquid in antiquam virtutem animosque virilis <sup>343</sup>et pater Aeneas et avunculus excitat Hector? <sup>344</sup>Talia fundebat lacrimans longosque ciebat <sup>345</sup>incassum fletus, cum sese a moenibus heros <sup>346</sup>Priamides multis Helenus comitantibus adfert, <sup>347</sup>adgnoscitque suos, laetusque ad limina ducit, <sup>348</sup>et multum lacrimas verba inter singula fundit. <sup>349</sup>Procedo, et parvam Troiam simulataque magnis <sup>350</sup>Pergama, et arentem Xanthi cognomine rivum
translation.text:
“‘Are you alive, O goddess-born?’ she said, ‘Or if a ghost, then where is Hector’s shade?’ At this, she cast a loud and frightful cry. With broken words I made this brief reply: ‘All of me that remains appears in sight; I live, if living be to loathe the light. No phantom; but I drag a wretched life, My fate resembling that of Hector’s wife. What have you suffer’d since you lost your lord? By what strange blessing are you now restor’d? Still are you Hector’s? or is Hector fled, And his remembrance lost in Pyrrhus’ bed?’ With eyes dejected, in a lowly tone, After a modest pause she thus begun:

“‘O only happy maid of Priam’s race, Whom death deliver’d from the foes’ embrace! Commanded on Achilles’ tomb to die, Not forc’d, like us, to hard captivity, Or in a haughty master’s arms to lie. In Grecian ships unhappy we were borne, Endur’d the victor’s lust, sustain’d the scorn: Thus I submitted to the lawless pride Of Pyrrhus, more a handmaid than a bride. Cloy’d with possession, he forsook my bed, And Helen’s lovely daughter sought to wed; Then me to Trojan Helenus resign’d, And his two slaves in equal marriage join’d; Till young Orestes, pierc’d with deep despair, And longing to redeem the promis’d fair, Before Apollo’s altar slew the ravisher. By Pyrrhus’ death the kingdom we regain’d: At least one half with Helenus remain’d. Our part, from Chaon, he Chaonia calls, And names from Pergamus his rising walls. But you, what fates have landed on our coast? What gods have sent you, or what storms have toss’d? Does young Ascanius life and health enjoy, Sav’d from the ruins of unhappy Troy? O tell me how his mother’s loss he bears, What hopes are promis’d from his blooming years, How much of Hector in his face appears?’ She spoke; and mix’d her speech with mournful cries, And fruitless tears came trickling from her eyes.

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>adgnosco, Scaeaeque amplector limina portae. <sup>352</sup>Nec non et Teucri socia simul urbe fruuntur: <sup>353</sup>illos porticibus rex accipiebat in amplis; <sup>354</sup>aulaï medio libabant pocula Bacchi, <sup>355</sup>impositis auro dapibus, paterasque tenebant. <sup>356</sup>Iamque dies alterque dies processit, et aurae <sup>357</sup>vela vocant tumidoque inflatur carbasus austro. <sup>358</sup>His vatem adgredior dictis ac talia quaeso: <sup>359</sup>Troiugena, interpres divom, qui numina Phoebi, <sup>360</sup>qui tripodas, Clarii laurus, qui sidera sentis, <sup>361</sup>et volucrum linguas et praepetis omina pennae, <sup>362</sup>fare age—namque omnem cursum mihi prospera dixit <sup>363</sup>religio, et cuncti suaserunt numine divi <sup>364</sup>Italiam petere et terras temptare repostas: <sup>365</sup>sola novum dictuque nefas Harpyia Celaeno <sup>366</sup>prodigium canit, et tristis denuntiat iras, <sup>367</sup>obscenamque famem—quae prima pericula vito? <sup>368</sup>Quidve sequens tantos possim superare labores? <sup>369</sup>Hic Helenus, caesis primum de more iuvencis, <sup>370</sup>exorat pacem divom, vittasque resolvit <sup>371</sup>sacrati capitis, meque ad tua limina, Phoebe, <sup>372</sup>ipse manu multo suspensum numine ducit, <sup>373</sup>atque haec deinde canit divino ex ore sacerdos: <sup>374</sup>Nate dea,—nam te maioribus ire per altum <sup>375</sup>auspiciis manifesta fides: sic fata deum rex
translation.text: “At length her lord descends upon the plain, In pomp, attended with a num’rous train; Receives his friends, and to the city leads, And tears of joy amidst his welcome sheds. Proceeding on, another Troy I see, Or, in less compass, Troy’s epitome. A riv’let by the name of Xanthus ran, And I embrace the Scaean gate again. My friends in porticoes were entertain’d, And feasts and pleasures thro’ the city reign’d. The tables fill’d the spacious hall around, And golden bowls with sparkling wine were crown’d. Two days we pass’d in mirth, till friendly gales, Blown from the south supplied our swelling sails. Then to the royal seer I thus began: ‘O thou, who know’st, beyond the reach of man, The laws of heav’n, and what the stars decree; Whom Phoebus taught unerring prophecy, From his own tripod, and his holy tree; Skill’d in the wing’d inhabitants of air, What auspices their notes and flights declare: O say; for all religious rites portend A happy voyage, and a prosp’rous end; And ev’ry power and omen of the sky Direct my course for destin’d Italy; But only dire Celaeno, from the gods, A dismal famine fatally forebodes: O say what dangers I am first to shun, What toils vanquish, and what course to run.’

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>sortitur, volvitque vices; is vertitur ordo— <sup>377</sup>pauca tibi e multis, quo tutior hospita lustres <sup>378</sup>aequora et Ausonio possis considere portu, <sup>379</sup>expediam dictis; prohibent nam cetera Parcae <sup>380</sup>scire Helenum farique vetat Saturnia Iuno. <sup>381</sup>Principio Italiam, quam tu iam rere propinquam <sup>382</sup>vicinosque, ignare, paras invadere portus, <sup>383</sup>longa procul longis via dividit invia terris. <sup>384</sup>Ante et Trinacria lentandus remus in unda, <sup>385</sup>et salis Ausonii lustrandum navibus aequor, <sup>386</sup>infernique lacus, Aeaeaeque insula Circae, <sup>387</sup>quam tuta possis urbem componere terra: <sup>388</sup>signa tibi dicam, tu condita mente teneto: <sup>389</sup>cum tibi sollicito secreti ad fluminis undam <sup>390</sup>litoreis ingens inventa sub ilicibus sus <sup>391</sup>triginta capitum fetus enixa iacebit. <sup>392</sup>alba, solo recubans, albi circum ubera nati, <sup>393</sup>is locus urbis erit, requies ea certa laborum. <sup>394</sup>Nec tu mensarum morsus horresce futuros: <sup>395</sup>fata viam invenient, aderitque vocatus Apollo. <sup>396</sup>Has autem terras, Italique hanc litoris oram, <sup>397</sup>proxuma quae nostri perfunditur aequoris aestu, <sup>398</sup>effuge; cuncta malis habitantur moenia Grais. <sup>399</sup>Hic et Narycii posuerunt moenia Locri, <sup>400</sup>et Sallentinos obsedit milite campos
translation.text: “The prophet first with sacrifice adores The greater gods; their pardon then implores; Unbinds the fillet from his holy head; To Phoebus, next, my trembling steps he led, Full of religious doubts and awful dread. Then, with his god possess’d, before the shrine, These words proceeded from his mouth divine: ‘O goddess-born, (for Heav’n’s appointed will, With greater auspices of good than ill, Foreshows thy voyage, and thy course directs; Thy fates conspire, and Jove himself protects,) Of many things some few I shall explain, Teach thee to shun the dangers of the main, And how at length the promis’d shore to gain. The rest the fates from Helenus conceal, And Juno’s angry pow’r forbids to tell. First, then, that happy shore, that seems so nigh, Will far from your deluded wishes fly; Long tracts of seas divide your hopes from Italy: For you must cruise along Sicilian shores, And stem the currents with your struggling oars; Then round th’ Italian coast your navy steer; And, after this, to Circe’s island veer; And, last, before your new foundations rise, Must pass the Stygian lake, and view the nether skies. Now mark the signs of future ease and rest, And bear them safely treasur’d in thy breast. When, in the shady shelter of a wood, And near the margin of a gentle flood, Thou shalt behold a sow upon the ground, With thirty sucking young encompass’d round; The dam and offspring white as falling snow: These on thy city shall their name bestow, And there shall end thy labours and thy woe. Nor let the threaten’d famine fright thy mind, For Phoebus will assist, and Fate the way will find. Let not thy course to that ill coast be bent, Which fronts from far th’ Epirian continent: Those parts are all by Grecian foes possess’d; The salvage Locrians here the shores infest; There fierce Idomeneus his city builds, And guards with arms the Salentinian fields; And on the mountain’s brow Petilia stands, Which Philoctetes with his troops commands. Ev’n when thy fleet is landed on the shore, And priests with holy vows the gods adore, Then with a purple veil involve your eyes, Lest hostile faces blast the sacrifice. These rites and customs to the rest commend, That to your pious race they may descend.

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>Lyctius Idomeneus; hic illa ducis Meliboei <sup>402</sup>parva Philoctetae subnixa Petelia muro. <sup>403</sup>Quin, ubi transmissae steterint trans aequora classes, <sup>404</sup>et positis aris iam vota in litore solves, <sup>405</sup>purpureo velare comas adopertus amictu, <sup>406</sup>ne qua inter sanctos ignis in honore deorum <sup>407</sup>hostilis facies occurrat et omina turbet. <sup>408</sup>Hunc socii morem sacrorum, hunc ipse teneto: <sup>409</sup>hac casti maneant in religione nepotes. <sup>410</sup>Ast ubi digressum Siculae te admoverit orae <sup>411</sup>ventus, et angusti rarescent claustra Pelori, <sup>412</sup>laeva tibi tellus et longo laeva petantur <sup>413</sup>aequora circuitu: dextrum fuge litus et undas. <sup>414</sup>Haec loca vi quondam et vasta convolsa ruina— <sup>415</sup>tantum aevi longinqua valet mutare vetustas— <sup>416</sup>dissiluisse ferunt, cum protinus utraque tellus <sup>417</sup>una foret; venit medio vi pontus et undis <sup>418</sup>Hesperium Siculo latus abscidit, arvaque et urbes <sup>419</sup>litore diductas angusto interluit aestu. <sup>420</sup>Dextrum Scylla latus, laevum implacata Charybdis <sup>421</sup>obsidet, atque imo barathri ter gurgite vastos <sup>422</sup>sorbet in abruptum fluctus, rursusque sub auras <sup>423</sup>erigit alternos et sidera verberat unda. <sup>424</sup>At Scyllam caecis cohibet spelunca latebris, <sup>425</sup>ora exsertantem et navis in saxa trahentem.
translation.text: ‘When, parted hence, the wind, that ready waits For Sicily, shall bear you to the straits Where proud Pelorus opes a wider way, Tack to the larboard, and stand off to sea: Veer starboard sea and land. Th’ Italian shore And fair Sicilia’s coast were one, before An earthquake caus’d the flaw: the roaring tides The passage broke that land from land divides; And where the lands retir’d, the rushing ocean rides. Distinguish’d by the straits, on either hand, Now rising cities in long order stand, And fruitful fields: so much can time invade The mould’ring work that beauteous Nature made. Far on the right, her dogs foul Scylla hides: Charybdis roaring on the left presides, And in her greedy whirlpool sucks the tides; Then spouts them from below: with fury driv’n, The waves mount up and wash the face of heav’n. But Scylla from her den, with open jaws, The sinking vessel in her eddy draws, Then dashes on the rocks. A human face, And virgin bosom, hides her tail’s disgrace: Her parts obscene below the waves descend, With dogs inclos’d, and in a dolphin end. ’Tis safer, then, to bear aloof to sea, And coast Pachynus, tho’ with more delay, Than once to view misshapen Scylla near, And the loud yell of wat’ry wolves to hear.

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>Prima hominis facies et pulchro pectore virgo <sup>427</sup>pube tenus, postrema immani corpore pristis, <sup>428</sup>delphinum caudas utero commissa luporum. <sup>429</sup>Praestat Trinacrii metas lustrare Pachyni <sup>430</sup>cessantem, longos et circumflectere cursus, <sup>431</sup>quam semel informem vasto vidisse sub antro <sup>432</sup>Scyllam, et caeruleis canibus resonantia saxa. <sup>433</sup>Praeterea, si qua est Heleno prudentia, vati <sup>434</sup>si qua fides, animum si veris implet Apollo, <sup>435</sup>unum illud tibi, nate dea, proque omnibus unum <sup>436</sup>praedicam, et repetens iterumque iterumque monebo: <sup>437</sup>Iunonis magnae primum prece numen adora; <sup>438</sup>Iunoni cane vota libens, dominamque potentem <sup>439</sup>supplicibus supera donis: sic denique victor <sup>440</sup>Trinacria finis Italos mittere relicta. <sup>441</sup>Huc ubi delatus Cumaeam accesseris urbem, <sup>442</sup>divinosque lacus, et Averna sonantia silvis, <sup>443</sup>insanam vatem aspicies, quae rupe sub ima <sup>444</sup>fata canit, foliisque notas et nomina mandat. <sup>445</sup>Quaecumque in foliis descripsit carmina virgo, <sup>446</sup>digerit in numerum, atque antro seclusa relinquit. <sup>447</sup>Illa manent immota locis, neque ab ordine cedunt; <sup>448</sup>verum eadem, verso tenuis cum cardine ventus <sup>449</sup>impulit et teneras turbavit ianua frondes, <sup>450</sup>numquam deinde cavo volitantia prendere saxo,
translation.text: “‘Besides, if faith to Helenus be due, And if prophetic Phoebus tell me true, Do not this precept of your friend forget, Which therefore more than once I must repeat: Above the rest, great Juno’s name adore; Pay vows to Juno; Juno’s aid implore. Let gifts be to the mighty queen design’d, And mollify with pray’rs her haughty mind. Thus, at the length, your passage shall be free, And you shall safe descend on Italy. Arriv’d at Cumae, when you view the flood Of black Avernus, and the sounding wood, The mad prophetic Sibyl you shall find, Dark in a cave, and on a rock reclin’d. She sings the fates, and, in her frantic fits, The notes and names, inscrib’d, to leafs commits. What she commits to leafs, in order laid, Before the cavern’s entrance are display’d: Unmov’d they lie; but, if a blast of wind Without, or vapours issue from behind, The leafs are borne aloft in liquid air, And she resumes no more her museful care, Nor gathers from the rocks her scatter’d verse, Nor sets in order what the winds disperse. Thus, many not succeeding, most upbraid The madness of the visionary maid, And with loud curses leave the mystic shade.

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>nec revocare situs aut iungere carmina curat: <sup>452</sup>inconsulti abeunt, sedemque odere Sibyllae. <sup>453</sup>Hic tibi ne qua morae fuerint dispendia tanti,— <sup>454</sup>quamvis increpitent socii, et vi cursus in altum <sup>455</sup>vela vocet, possisque sinus implere secundos,— <sup>456</sup>quin adeas vatem precibusque oracula poscas <sup>457</sup>ipsa canat, vocemque volens atque ora resolvat. <sup>458</sup>Illa tibi Italiae populos venturaque bella, <sup>459</sup>et quo quemque modo fugiasque ferasque laborem <sup>460</sup>expediet, cursusque dabit venerata secundos. <sup>461</sup>Haec sunt, quae nostra liceat te voce moneri. <sup>462</sup>Vade age, et ingentem factis fer ad aethera Troiam. <sup>463</sup>Quae postquam vates sic ore effatus amico est, <sup>464</sup>dona dehinc auro gravia sectoque elephanto <sup>465</sup>imperat ad navis ferri, stipatque carinis <sup>466</sup>ingens argentum, Dodonaeosque lebetas, <sup>467</sup>loricam consertam hamis auroque trilicem, <sup>468</sup>et conum insignis galeae cristasque comantis, <sup>469</sup>arma Neoptolemi; sunt et sua dona parenti. <sup>470</sup>Addit equos, additque duces; <sup>471</sup>remigium supplet; socios simul instruit armis. <sup>472</sup>Interea classem velis aptare iubebat <sup>473</sup>Anchises, fieret vento mora ne qua ferenti. <sup>474</sup>Quem Phoebi interpres multo compellat honore: <sup>475</sup>Coniugio, Anchise, Veneris dignate superbo,
translation.text:
“‘Think it not loss of time a while to stay, Tho’ thy companions chide thy long delay; Tho’ summon’d to the seas, tho’ pleasing gales Invite thy course, and stretch thy swelling sails: But beg the sacred priestess to relate With willing words, and not to write thy fate. The fierce Italian people she will show, And all thy wars, and all thy future woe, And what thou may’st avoid, and what must undergo. She shall direct thy course, instruct thy mind, And teach thee how the happy shores to find. This is what Heav’n allows me to relate: Now part in peace; pursue thy better fate, And raise, by strength of arms, the Trojan state.’

“This when the priest with friendly voice declar’d, He gave me license, and rich gifts prepar’d: Bounteous of treasure, he supplied my want With heavy gold, and polish’d elephant; Then Dodonaean caldrons put on board, And ev’ry ship with sums of silver stor’d. A trusty coat of mail to me he sent, Thrice chain’d with gold, for use and ornament; The helm of Pyrrhus added to the rest, That flourish’d with a plume and waving crest. Nor was my sire forgotten, nor my friends; And large recruits he to my navy sends: Men, horses, captains, arms, and warlike stores; Supplies new pilots, and new sweeping oars. Meantime, my sire commands to hoist our sails, Lest we should lose the first auspicious gales.

translation.original_text: <sup>476</sup>cura deum, bis Pergameis erepte ruinis, <sup>477</sup>ecce tibi Ausoniae tellus; hanc arripe velis. <sup>478</sup>Et tamen hanc pelago praeterlabare necesse est; <sup>479</sup>Ausoniae pars illa procul, quam pandit Apollo. <sup>480</sup>Vade ait O felix nati pietate.  Quid ultra <sup>481</sup>provehor, et fando surgentis demoror austros? <sup>482</sup>Nec minus Andromache digressu maesta supremo <sup>483</sup>fert picturatas auri subtemine vestes <sup>484</sup>et Phrygiam Ascanio chlamydem (nec cedit honore), <sup>485</sup>textilibusque onerat donis, ac talia fatur: <sup>486</sup>Accipe et haec, manuum tibi quae monumenta mearum <sup>487</sup>sint, puer, et longum Andromachae testentur amorem, <sup>488</sup>coniugis Hectoreae.  Cape dona extrema tuorum, <sup>489</sup>O mihi sola mei super Astyanactis imago: <sup>490</sup>sic oculos, sic ille manus, sic ora ferebat; <sup>491</sup>et nunc aequali tecum pubesceret aevo. <sup>492</sup>Hos ego digrediens lacrimis adfabar obortis: <sup>493</sup>Vivite felices, quibus est fortuna peracta <sup>494</sup>iam sua; nos alia ex aliis in fata vocamur. <sup>495</sup>Vobis parta quies; nullum maris aequor arandum, <sup>496</sup>arva neque Ausoniae semper cedentia retro <sup>497</sup>quaerenda.  Effigiem Xanthi Troiamque videtis <sup>498</sup>quam vestrae fecere manus, melioribus, opto, <sup>499</sup>auspiciis, et quae fuerit minus obvia Graiis. <sup>500</sup>Si quando Thybrim vicinaque Thybridis arva
translation.text: “The prophet bless’d the parting crew, and last, With words like these, his ancient friend embrac’d: ‘Old happy man, the care of gods above, Whom heav’nly Venus honour’d with her love, And twice preserv’d thy life, when Troy was lost, Behold from far the wish’d Ausonian coast: There land; but take a larger compass round, For that before is all forbidden ground. The shore that Phoebus has design’d for you, At farther distance lies, conceal’d from view. Go happy hence, and seek your new abodes, Blest in a son, and favour’d by the gods: For I with useless words prolong your stay, When southern gales have summon’d you away.’

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>intraro, gentique meae data moenia cernam, <sup>502</sup>cognatas urbes olim populosque propinquos, <sup>503</sup>Epiro, Hesperia, quibus idem Dardanus auctor <sup>504</sup>atque idem casus, unam faciemus utramque <sup>505</sup>Troiam animis; maneat nostros ea cura nepotes. <sup>506</sup>Provehimur pelago vicina Ceraunia iuxta, <sup>507</sup>unde iter Italiam cursusque brevissimus undis. <sup>508</sup>Sol ruit interea et montes umbrantur opaci; <sup>509</sup>sternimur optatae gremio telluris ad undam, <sup>510</sup>sortiti remos, passimque in litore sicco <sup>511</sup>corpora curamus; fessos sopor inrigat artus. <sup>512</sup>Necdum orbem medium Nox horis acta subibat: <sup>513</sup>haud segnis strato surgit Palinurus et omnis <sup>514</sup>explorat ventos, atque auribus aera captat; <sup>515</sup>sidera cuncta notat tacito labentia caelo, <sup>516</sup>Arcturum pluviasque Hyadas geminosque Triones, <sup>517</sup>armatumque auro circumspicit Oriona. <sup>518</sup>Postquam cuncta videt caelo constare sereno, <sup>519</sup>dat clarum e puppi signum; nos castra movemus, <sup>520</sup>temptamusque viam et velorum pandimus alas. <sup>521</sup>Iamque rubescebat stellis Aurora fugatis, <sup>522</sup>cum procul obscuros collis humilemque videmus <sup>523</sup>Italiam.  Italiam primus conclamat Achates, <sup>524</sup>Italiam laeto socii clamore salutant. <sup>525</sup>Tum pater Anchises magnum cratera corona
translation.text: “Nor less the queen our parting thence deplor’d, Nor was less bounteous than her Trojan lord. A noble present to my son she brought, A robe with flow’rs on golden tissue wrought, A phrygian vest; and loads with gifts beside Of precious texture, and of Asian pride. ‘Accept,’ she said, ‘these monuments of love, Which in my youth with happier hands I wove: Regard these trifles for the giver’s sake; ’Tis the last present Hector’s wife can make. Thou call’st my lost Astyanax to mind; In thee his features and his form I find: His eyes so sparkled with a lively flame; Such were his motions; such was all his frame; And ah! had Heav’n so pleas’d, his years had been the same.’

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>induit, implevitque mero, divosque vocavit <sup>527</sup>stans celsa in puppi: <sup>528</sup>Di maris et terrae tempestatumque potentes, <sup>529</sup>ferte viam vento facilem et spirate secundi. <sup>530</sup>Crebrescunt optatae aurae portusque patescit <sup>531</sup>iam propior, templumque adparet in arce Minervae. <sup>532</sup>Vela legunt socii et proras ad litora torquent. <sup>533</sup>Portus ab Euroo fluctu curvatus in arcum, <sup>534</sup>obiectae salsa spumant aspargine cautes; <sup>535</sup>ipse latet; gemino demittunt bracchia muro <sup>536</sup>turriti scopuli, refugitque ab litore templum. <sup>537</sup>Quattuor hic, primum omen, equos in gramine vidi <sup>538</sup>tondentis campum late, candore nivali. <sup>539</sup>Et pater Anchises: Bellum, O terra hospita, portas <sup>540</sup>bello armantur equi, bellum haec armenta minantur. <sup>541</sup>Sed tamen idem olim curru succedere sueti <sup>542</sup>quadrupedes, et frena iugo concordia ferre; <sup>543</sup>spes et pacis ait.  Tum numina sancta precamur <sup>544</sup>Palladis armisonae, quae prima accepit ovantis, <sup>545</sup>et capita ante aras Phrygio velamur amictu; <sup>546</sup>praeceptisque Heleni, dederat quae maxima, rite <sup>547</sup>Iunoni Argivae iussos adolemus honores. <sup>548</sup>Haud mora, continuo perfectis ordine votis, <sup>549</sup>cornua velatarum obvertimus antemarum, <sup>550</sup>Graiugenumque domos suspectaque linquimus arva.
translation.text: “With tears I took my last adieu, and said: ‘Your fortune, happy pair, already made, Leaves you no farther wish. My diff’rent state, Avoiding one, incurs another fate. To you a quiet seat the gods allow: You have no shores to search, no seas to plow, Nor fields of flying Italy to chase: (Deluding visions, and a vain embrace!) You see another Simois, and enjoy The labour of your hands, another Troy, With better auspice than her ancient tow’rs, And less obnoxious to the Grecian pow’rs. If e’er the gods, whom I with vows adore, Conduct my steps to Tiber’s happy shore; If ever I ascend the Latian throne, And build a city I may call my own; As both of us our birth from Troy derive, So let our kindred lines in concord live, And both in acts of equal friendship strive. Our fortunes, good or bad, shall be the same: The double Troy shall differ but in name; That what we now begin may never end, But long to late posterity descend.’

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>Hinc sinus Herculei (si vera est fama) Tarenti <sup>552</sup>cernitur; attollit se diva Lacinia contra, <sup>553</sup>Caulonisque arces et navifragum Scylaceum. <sup>554</sup>Tum procul e fluctu Trinacria cernitur Aetna, <sup>555</sup>et gemitum ingentem pelagi pulsataque saxa <sup>556</sup>audimus longe fractasque ad litora voces, <sup>557</sup>exsultantque vada, atque aestu miscentur harenae. <sup>558</sup>Et pater Anchises: Nimirum haec illa Charybdis: <sup>559</sup>hos Helenus scopulos, haec saxa horrenda canebat. <sup>560</sup>Eripite, O socii, pariterque insurgite remis! <sup>561</sup>Haud minus ac iussi faciunt, primusque rudentem <sup>562</sup>contorsit laevas proram Palinurus ad undas. <sup>563</sup>laevam cuncta cohors remis ventisque petivit. <sup>564</sup>Tollimur in caelum curvato gurgite, et idem <sup>565</sup>subducta ad Manis imos desedimus unda. <sup>566</sup>Ter scopuli clamorem inter cava saxa dedere: <sup>567</sup>ter spumam elisam et rorantia vidimus astra. <sup>568</sup>Interea fessos ventus cum sole reliquit, <sup>569</sup>ignarique viae Cyclopum adlabimur oris. <sup>570</sup>Portus ab accessu ventorum immotus et ingens <sup>571</sup>ipse; sed horrificis iuxta tonat Aetna ruinis; <sup>572</sup>interdumque atram prorumpit ad aethera nubem, <sup>573</sup>turbine fumantem piceo et candente favilla, <sup>574</sup>attollitque globos flammarum et sidera lambit; <sup>575</sup>interdum scopulos avolsaque viscera montis
translation.text:
“Near the Ceraunian rocks our course we bore; The shortest passage to th’ Italian shore. Now had the sun withdrawn his radiant light, And hills were hid in dusky shades of night: We land, and, on the bosom of the ground, A safe retreat and a bare lodging found. Close by the shore we lay; the sailors keep Their watches, and the rest securely sleep. The night, proceeding on with silent pace, Stood in her noon, and view’d with equal face Her steepy rise and her declining race. Then wakeful Palinurus rose, to spy The face of heav’n, and the nocturnal sky; And listen’d ev’ry breath of air to try; Observes the stars, and notes their sliding course, The Pleiads, Hyads, and their wat’ry force; And both the Bears is careful to behold, And bright Orion, arm’d with burnish’d gold. Then, when he saw no threat’ning tempest nigh, But a sure promise of a settled sky, He gave the sign to weigh; we break our sleep, Forsake the pleasing shore, and plow the deep.

“And now the rising morn with rosy light Adorns the skies, and puts the stars to flight; When we from far, like bluish mists, descry The hills, and then the plains, of Italy. Achates first pronounc’d the joyful sound; Then, ‘Italy!’ the cheerful crew rebound. My sire Anchises crown’d a cup with wine, And, off’ring, thus implor’d the pow’rs divine: ‘Ye gods, presiding over lands and seas, And you who raging winds and waves appease, Breathe on our swelling sails a prosp’rous wind, And smooth our passage to the port assign’d!’ The gentle gales their flagging force renew, And now the happy harbour is in view. Minerva’s temple then salutes our sight, Plac’d, as a landmark, on the mountain’s height. We furl our sails, and turn the prows to shore; The curling waters round the galleys roar. The land lies open to the raging east, Then, bending like a bow, with rocks compress’d, Shuts out the storms; the winds and waves complain, And vent their malice on the cliffs in vain. The port lies hid within; on either side Two tow’ring rocks the narrow mouth divide. The temple, which aloft we view’d before, To distance flies, and seems to shun the shore. Scarce landed, the first omens I beheld Were four white steeds that cropp’d the flow’ry field. ‘War, war is threaten’d from this foreign ground,’ My father cried, ‘where warlike steeds are found. Yet, since reclaim’d to chariots they submit, And bend to stubborn yokes, and champ the bit, Peace may succeed to war.’ Our way we bend To Pallas, and the sacred hill ascend; There prostrate to the fierce Virago pray, Whose temple was the landmark of our way. Each with a Phrygian mantle veil’d his head, And all commands of Helenus obey’d, And pious rites to Grecian Juno paid. These dues perform’d, we stretch our sails, and stand To sea, forsaking that suspected land.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>erigit eructans, liquefactaque saxa sub auras <sup>577</sup>cum gemitu glomerat, fundoque exaestuat imo. <sup>578</sup>Fama est Enceladi semustum fulmine corpus <sup>579</sup>urgueri mole hac, ingentemque insuper Aetnam <sup>580</sup>impositam ruptis flammam exspirare caminis; <sup>581</sup>et fessum quotiens mutet latus, intremere omnem <sup>582</sup>murmure Trinacriam, et caelum subtexere fumo. <sup>583</sup>Noctem illam tecti silvis immania monstra <sup>584</sup>perferimus, nec quae sonitum det causa videmus. <sup>585</sup>Nam neque erant astrorum ignes, nec lucidus aethra <sup>586</sup>siderea polus, obscuro sed nubila caelo, <sup>587</sup>et lunam in nimbo nox intempesta tenebat. <sup>588</sup>Postera iamque dies primo surgebat Eoo, <sup>589</sup>umentemque Aurora polo dimoverat umbram: <sup>590</sup>cum subito e silvis, macie confecta suprema, <sup>591</sup>ignoti nova forma viri miserandaque cultu <sup>592</sup>procedit, supplexque manus ad litora tendit. <sup>593</sup>Respicimus: dira inluvies inmissaque barba, <sup>594</sup>consertum tegumen spinis; at cetera Graius, <sup>595</sup>et quondam patriis ad Troiam missus in armis. <sup>596</sup>Isque ubi Dardanios habitus et Troia vidit <sup>597</sup>arma procul, paulum aspectu conterritus haesit, <sup>598</sup>continuitque gradum; mox sese ad litora praeceps <sup>599</sup>cum fletu precibusque tulit: Per sidera testor, <sup>600</sup>per superos atque hoc caeli spirabile lumen,
translation.text: “From hence Tarentum’s bay appears in view, For Hercules renown’d, if fame be true. Just opposite, Lacinian Juno stands; Caulonian tow’rs, and Scylacaean strands, For shipwrecks fear’d. Mount Aetna thence we spy, Known by the smoky flames which cloud the sky. Far off we hear the waves with surly sound Invade the rocks, the rocks their groans rebound. The billows break upon the sounding strand, And roll the rising tide, impure with sand. Then thus Anchises, in experience old: ‘’Tis that Charybdis which the seer foretold, And those the promis’d rocks! Bear off to sea!’ With haste the frighted mariners obey. First Palinurus to the larboard veer’d; Then all the fleet by his example steer’d. To heav’n aloft on ridgy waves we ride, Then down to hell descend, when they divide; And thrice our galleys knock’d the stony ground, And thrice the hollow rocks return’d the sound, And thrice we saw the stars, that stood with dews around. The flagging winds forsook us, with the sun; And, wearied, on Cyclopian shores we run. The port capacious, and secure from wind, Is to the foot of thund’ring Aetna join’d. By turns a pitchy cloud she rolls on high; By turns hot embers from her entrails fly, And flakes of mounting flames, that lick the sky. Oft from her bowels massy rocks are thrown, And, shiver’d by the force, come piecemeal down. Oft liquid lakes of burning sulphur flow, Fed from the fiery springs that boil below. Enceladus, they say, transfix’d by Jove, With blasted limbs came tumbling from above; And, where he fell, th’ avenging father drew This flaming hill, and on his body threw. As often as he turns his weary sides, He shakes the solid isle, and smoke the heavens hides. In shady woods we pass the tedious night, Where bellowing sounds and groans our souls affright, Of which no cause is offer’d to the sight; For not one star was kindled in the sky, Nor could the moon her borrow’d light supply; For misty clouds involv’d the firmament, The stars were muffled, and the moon was pent.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>tollite me, Teucri; quascumque abducite terras; <sup>602</sup>hoc sat erit.  Scio me Danais e classibus unum, <sup>603</sup>et bello Iliacos fateor petiisse Penatis; <sup>604</sup>pro quo, si sceleris tanta est iniuria nostri, <sup>605</sup>spargite me in fluctus, vastoque inmergite ponto. <sup>606</sup>Si pereo, hominum manibus periisse iuvabit. <sup>607</sup>Dixerat, et genua amplexus genibusque volutans <sup>608</sup>haerebat.  Qui sit, fari, quo sanguine cretus, <sup>609</sup>hortamur; quae deinde agitet fortuna, fateri. <sup>610</sup>Ipse pater dextram Anchises, haud multa moratus, <sup>611</sup>dat iuveni, atque animum praesenti pignore firmat. <sup>612</sup>Ille haec, deposita tandem formidine, fatur: <sup>613</sup>Sum patria ex Ithaca, comes infelicis Ulixi, <sup>614</sup>nomine Achaemenides, Troiam genitore Adamasto <sup>615</sup>paupere—mansissetque utinam fortuna!—profectus. <sup>616</sup>Hic me, dum trepidi crudelia limina linquunt, <sup>617</sup>inmemores socii vasto Cyclopis in antro <sup>618</sup>deseruere.  Domus sanie dapibusque cruentis, <sup>619</sup>intus opaca, ingens; ipse arduus, altaque pulsat <sup>620</sup>sidera—Di, talem terris avertite pestem!— <sup>621</sup>nec visu facilis nec dictu adfabilis ulli. <sup>622</sup>Visceribus miserorum et sanguine vescitur atro. <sup>623</sup>Vidi egomet, duo de numero cum corpora nostro <sup>624</sup>prensa manu magna, medio resupinus in antro, <sup>625</sup>frangeret ad saxum, sanieque aspersa natarent
translation.text: “Scarce had the rising sun the day reveal’d, Scarce had his heat the pearly dews dispell’d, When from the woods there bolts, before our sight, Somewhat betwixt a mortal and a sprite, So thin, so ghastly meager, and so wan, So bare of flesh, he scarce resembled man. This thing, all tatter’d, seem’d from far t’implore Our pious aid, and pointed to the shore. We look behind, then view his shaggy beard; His clothes were tagg’d with thorns, and filth his limbs besmear’d; The rest, in mien, in habit, and in face, Appear’d a Greek, and such indeed he was. He cast on us, from far, a frightful view, Whom soon for Trojans and for foes he knew; Stood still, and paus’d; then all at once began To stretch his limbs, and trembled as he ran. Soon as approach’d, upon his knees he falls, And thus with tears and sighs for pity calls: ‘Now, by the pow’rs above, and what we share From Nature’s common gift, this vital air, O Trojans, take me hence! I beg no more; But bear me far from this unhappy shore. ’Tis true, I am a Greek, and farther own, Among your foes besieg’d th’ imperial town. For such demerits if my death be due, No more for this abandon’d life I sue; This only favour let my tears obtain, To throw me headlong in the rapid main: Since nothing more than death my crime demands, I die content, to die by human hands.’ He said, and on his knees my knees embrac’d: I bade him boldly tell his fortune past, His present state, his lineage, and his name, Th’ occasion of his fears, and whence he came. The good Anchises rais’d him with his hand; Who, thus encourag’d, answer’d our demand: ‘From Ithaca, my native soil, I came To Troy; and Achaemenides my name. Me my poor father with Ulysses sent; (O had I stay’d, with poverty content!) But, fearful for themselves, my countrymen Left me forsaken in the Cyclops’ den. The cave, tho’ large, was dark; the dismal floor Was pav’d with mangled limbs and putrid gore. Our monstrous host, of more than human size, Erects his head, and stares within the skies; Bellowing his voice, and horrid is his hue. Ye gods, remove this plague from mortal view! The joints of slaughter’d wretches are his food; And for his wine he quaffs the streaming blood. These eyes beheld, when with his spacious hand He seiz’d two captives of our Grecian band; Stretch’d on his back, he dash’d against the stones Their broken bodies, and their crackling bones: With spouting blood the purple pavement swims, While the dire glutton grinds the trembling limbs.

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>limina; vidi atro cum membra fluentia tabo <sup>627</sup>manderet, et tepidi tremerent sub dentibus artus. <sup>628</sup>Haud impune quidem; nec talia passus Ulixes, <sup>629</sup>oblitusve sui est Ithacus discrimine tanto. <sup>630</sup>Nam simul expletus dapibus vinoque sepultus <sup>631</sup>cervicem inflexam posuit, iacuitque per antrum <sup>632</sup>immensus, saniem eructans et frusta cruento <sup>633</sup>per somnum commixta mero, nos magna precati <sup>634</sup>numina sortitique vices, una undique circum <sup>635</sup>fundimur, et telo lumen terebramus acuto,— <sup>636</sup>ingens, quod torva solum sub fronte latebat, <sup>637</sup>Argolici clipei aut Phoebeae lampadis instar,— <sup>638</sup>et tandem laeti sociorum ulciscimur umbras. <sup>639</sup>Sed fugite, O miseri, fugite, atque ab litore funem <sup>640</sup>rumpite. <sup>641</sup>Nam qualis quantusque cavo Polyphemus in antro <sup>642</sup>lanigeras claudit pecudes atque ubera pressat, <sup>643</sup>centum alii curva haec habitant ad litora volgo <sup>644</sup>infandi Cyclopes, et altis montibus errant. <sup>645</sup>Tertia iam lunae se cornua lumine complent, <sup>646</sup>cum vitam in silvis inter deserta ferarum <sup>647</sup>lustra domosque traho, vastosque ab rupe Cyclopas <sup>648</sup>prospicio, sonitumque pedum vocemque tremesco. <sup>649</sup>Victum infelicem, bacas lapidosaque corna, <sup>650</sup>dant rami et volsis pascunt radicibus herbae.
translation.text: “‘Not unreveng’d Ulysses bore their fate, Nor thoughtless of his own unhappy state; For, gorg’d with flesh, and drunk with human wine While fast asleep the giant lay supine, Snoring aloud, and belching from his maw His indigested foam, and morsels raw; We pray; we cast the lots, and then surround The monstrous body, stretch’d along the ground: Each, as he could approach him, lends a hand To bore his eyeball with a flaming brand. Beneath his frowning forehead lay his eye; For only one did the vast frame supply; But that a globe so large, his front it fill’d, Like the sun’s disk or like a Grecian shield. The stroke succeeds; and down the pupil bends: This vengeance follow’d for our slaughter’d friends. But haste, unhappy wretches, haste to fly! Your cables cut, and on your oars rely! Such, and so vast as Polypheme appears, A hundred more this hated island bears: Like him, in caves they shut their woolly sheep; Like him, their herds on tops of mountains keep; Like him, with mighty strides, they stalk from steep to steep And now three moons their sharpen’d horns renew, Since thus, in woods and wilds, obscure from view, I drag my loathsome days with mortal fright, And in deserted caverns lodge by night; Oft from the rocks a dreadful prospect see Of the huge Cyclops, like a walking tree: From far I hear his thund’ring voice resound, And trampling feet that shake the solid ground. Cornels and salvage berries of the wood, And roots and herbs, have been my meager food. While all around my longing eyes I cast, I saw your happy ships appear at last. On those I fix’d my hopes, to these I run; ’Tis all I ask, this cruel race to shun; What other death you please, yourselves bestow.’

translation.original_text: <sup>651</sup>Omnia conlustrans, hanc primum ad litora classem <sup>652</sup>conspexi venientem.
